text: Sighting the ports is a personal preference and depends on whether the surgeon wants to stand in front or at the back of the patient.
I prefer a posterior approach.
On introduction of the camera, one could see that the liver was herniating into the chest as expected.
The defect in the diaphragm is between the aponeurotic central tendon and the costal muscular origin of the diaphragm, consistent with the Bogdanovich hernia.
Note the absence of hernia sac over the liver.
Also note the small diaphragmatic fenestrations covered only by thin transparent pleura.
These fenestrations are thought to be important in the pathology of catamenia pneumothorax, especially in patients with endometriosis.
The operation is started by releasing the adhesions between the neck of the hernia and the liver using the ultrasonic energy device.
I made use of a retracting stitch and plegated suture.
As mentioned earlier, carbon dioxide pressure is monitored all the time and mustn't exceed 12 mmHg.
Carbon dioxide leak into the peritoneal cavity can distend the abdomen, compress on the contralateral diaphragm and compromise ventilation.
If this happens, all that needs doing is to allow gas to escape through one of the ports to reduce the pressures to the required levels.
There was no particular difficulty in dissecting the liver off the diaphragm as demonstrated in this video.
The use of the ultrasonic energy device is very useful in this procedure.
Once all the adhesions were released, the liver slipped back into its natural abdominal position.
The edges of the neck of the hernia are then approximated to ensure that direct repair is feasible without the need for a synthetic patch.
To perform an intraoperative crude phrenic nerve conduction test, the nerve is approached at the apex of the chest cavity at its course over the superior vena cava.

The tip of an endoscopic grasper connected to the diaphragm is positioned close to the nerve and a short burst of low energy electrical stimulus is given while it's observing the twitching on the diaphragm.
The hernia defect was closed with one line of interrupted horizontal mattress stitches fortified by Teflon pedgets to avoid tearing through the diaphragm muscles.
Five to six lock ties are needed before cutting the stitch.
Five or six interrupted stitches were required to close the hernia in one line.
This repair was buried into the abdomen by the diaphragmatic placation.
The placation was performed in a single radial line that runs from the cardio-phrenic angle to the chest wall laterally.
Unlike the first line of repair, direct vision into the peritoneal sac was lost.
However, by bunching, clenching and folding the diaphragm, it becomes highly unlikely to hitchhike any of the abdominal organs when taking these stitches.
Before judging the tightness of the repair, the carbon dioxide must be switched off.
At the end of the procedure, a single 24 French intercostal drain is inserted and the lung is allowed to expand.
An extra pleural catheter is introduced for post-operative analgesia.
